content.assessment: Through the Finnish reform lens, status quo maintenance is directly contrary to the core lesson of Finland's comprehensive-school transition: that delaying tracking and unifying provision reduced between-school variance and improved outcomes, though only after roughly two decades and alongside complementary reforms (F-live-finnish_reform-05). Hungary's current structure—tracking at ages 10-13, far earlier than the OECD norm and far earlier than Finland's post-reform single structure—sits at the pole of early-selection systems that cross-national evidence associates with greater inequality (F-live-finnish_reform-04). Monitoring without structural change does not engage the tracking-age or comprehensive-provision variables that the Finnish case identifies as central levers, and the political durability question is moot since no reform is attempted.
opportunities: Monitoring could in principle generate longitudinal data comparable to what enabled evaluation of Finland's reform trajectory over two decades, if sustained long enough
strengths: Avoids the disruption costs and political conflict that structural change entails, preserving current institutional stability
threats: Finland's own post-1990s experience shows that even comprehensive structures erode toward inequality via within-school selection and school-choice effects (F-live-finnish_reform-06); a system that has not even achieved the comprehensive baseline is exposed to compounding disadvantage from both early tracking and the erosion mechanisms Finland later faced
weaknesses: Does not address early tracking age (10-13), which the lens identifies as the primary inequality-driving mechanism relative to Finland's post-1972 single-structure model (F-live-finnish_reform-04, F-live-finnish_reform-05)
Fails to address comprehensive provision at all, leaving Hungary's system structurally opposite to the Finnish model that produced measurable equity gains
